content.full_text:
October 28, 1985


John David Warburton
1900 Salinas Road, Space 40
Watsonville, CA 95076

Re: Your Request for Advice
FPPC NO. A-85-214 - /

Dear Mr. Warburton:

This is in response to your letter of October 10, 1985,
requesting advice regarding the campaign disclosure provisions of
the Political Reform Act (Government Code Sections 81000-91015).
Specifically, you asked whether the activities of your
organization require you to file a Statement of Organization and
campaign disclosure reports pursuant to the Act.

In your letter, you state that the purpose of your
organization is to oppose a rezoning proposal for the Scotts
Valley General Plan which is being considered by the City Council,
and that there is no election in Scotts Valley during 1985, either
to vote on the rezoning proposal or to elect members to the City
Council. You provided several pieces of literature which your
organization has prepared and distributed to residents of Scotts
Valley encouraging them to contact the City Council and attend
City Council meetings to voice opposition to the rezoning proposal.
There is nothing in the literature which refers to or advocates
the election or defeat of a candidate or measure.

Government Code Section 84101 requires all recipient
committees to file a Statement of Organization. Section 82013(a)
defines recipient committee as any person or combination of
persons who "receives contributions totaling five hundred dollars
($500) or more in a calendar year."

In order for the funds received by your organization to be
considered "contributions," the organization must be using funds
to make "expenditures" as defined in FPPC regulation 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18225 (copy enclosed). Section 18225 states
that an "expenditure" is a payment made to or for the benefit of a
candidate or committee, or a payment for communications which
"expressly advocate the nomination, election or defeat of a
clearly identified candidate or candidates, or the qualification,
passage or defeat of a clearly identified ballot measure." The
regulation further defines the terms "clearly identified" and
"expressly advocates."


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

John David Warburton
October 28, 1985
Page 2


     Because the materials you provided contain nothing that
could be construed as expressly advocating the nomination,
election or defeat of a candidate or measure, payments made by
your organization are not "expenditures." Therefore, the payments
your organization receives from its members are not
"contributions" and you have no obligation to file a Statement of
Organization or campaign disclosure reports.

     Finally, you asked whether you can include certain material
in your pamphlets, such as copies of court documents and material
out of the General Plan. The Commission has no authority to
authorize or prohibit inclusion of such materials and I am not
aware of any other law which would prohibit it. However, you may
wish to contact your local district attorney for a definite answer.

     I hope the foregoing has been helpful. Please let me know
if I can be of any assistance in the future.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Carla Wardlow
                                        Political Reform Consultant

CW:kt

John David Warburton
19000 Salinas Rd. Space 40
Watsonville California 95076


Dear Persons
On 10/10/85 I phoned your office (Fair Political Practices Commission)
and I talked with one of your staff.I told them that I wanted
to know whether or not we should apply for a Statement of
Organization (Recipient Committee) number? through the Secretary
of States Office. Your staff member upon hearing our explanation
said that they were positive that we did'nt have to but if
we wanted to be sure we could send in our pamphlets and
that your office would review them and send us a letter
back telling us that either we would have to file for a
Statement of Organization or not. We are opposing one of the
proposals (rezoning proposals) of the City of Scotts Valley's
new General Plan. This Plan is yet to be adopted and we
the citizens cannot vote on it but it's or the outcome will
be decided by a vote of the City Council. There is no ballot
and no election of City Officials this year --not until[?]
no general election until next June (of 06). We ire (under
State Law) allowed to attend the hearings and to voice our
opinions, (input) we wonder however if our material (pamphlets)
could be construed as political campaigning (howbeit far
in advance) and if this would make it mandatory for us to
have a Statement of Organization.
     Time is of the essence to us and we would greatly
appreciate hearing from you prontly.

     Also we would like to know if we can include in our
pamphlets a zeroxed copy of a Superior Court record such (documents)
as the copy enclosed in this mailing. Please note we are
not asking or telling anyone to vote in the future for
any candidate and we are only interested at present in
bringing out the true facts concerning our new,(fairly new)
majority Council (government) and the restrictions that
it is trying to impose upon us.
     Also can we use copied material out of the new General
Plan book in our pamphlets as a matter of reference for example,
seeing as it was our tax dollars that paid for the Plan.
We would like to use maps etc. such as the one you will
notice in our golden or light brown pamphlet.
                    Thankyou very much
                    Sincerely yours
                                        Dave Warburton
                                        John David Warburton
                                        10/10/85

P.S. If we cannot use
copies of the court doc.
it would like to know why
please thankyou

MITCHELL PAGE
PAGE & COHEN
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
105 SOQUEL AVENUE
SANTA CRUZ, CALIFORNIA 95060
408/426-0466

(SPACE BELOW FOR FILING STAMP ONLY)

1

2

3

4

5    Attorneys for_______Petitioner

6

7

8

9         IN THE SUPERIOR COURT OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA

10         IN AND FOR THE COUNTY OF SANTA CRUZ

11    COMMITTEE FOR ORDERLY GROWTH,    )
      JOE MILLER and ROGER ANDERSON,   )  No.  84739
12                                       )
           Petitioners,                  )
13                                       )  NOTICE OF ENTRY OF JUDGMENT
14    vs.                                 )
                                         )
      CITY OF SCOTTS VALLEY,             )
15                                       )
           Respondents.                  )
16    ___________________________________)

17    TO:  CITY OF SCOTTS VALLEY and to its attorney of record herein:

18         PLEASE TAKE NOTICE that on April 15, 1985, judgment was

19    entered in favor of Petitioner and against Respondent for

20    $13,193.00 in attorney's fees.

21         Dated:__4/25/85_     _____________________________
                                MITCHELL PAGE, Attorney for
22                              Petitioners

c.

The necessity and financial
burden of enforcement are such
as to make the award appropriate.

There can be no question but that the necessity of private
enforcement was clear in this case. As the Supreme Court stated
in Woodland Hills II: "Inasmuch as the present action proceeded
against the only governmental agencies that bear responsibility
for [in that case, subdivision approval], the necessity of private,
as compared to public, enforcement becomes clear." Woodland Hills
II, supra, at 941. As in that case, this litigation was brought
against the only public agency specifically charged by law with the
responsibility for adoption of a general plan for Scotts Valley
in compliance with State law.

It is equally clear that the financial burden of private
enforcement in this case is such as to make an attorney's fee award
appropriate. With regard to this aspect of C.C.P. §1021.5, the
Supreme Court has stated:

An award on the "private attorney general" theory
is appropriate when the cost of claimant's legal
victory transcends his personal interest, that is
when the necessity for pursuing the lawsuit placed
a burden on the plaintiff "out of proportion" to his
individual stake in the matter. [Cit.] Woodland Hills
II, supra, at 941

In the instant case, the individual Petitioners have no
personal financial interest in the matter. Plaintiff Committee
for Orderly Growth is an association of citizens and residents of
the City of Scotts Valley who are devoted to ensuring that growth
and development in the City occur in an orderly manner, and
(Plaintiffs Roger Anderson and Joe Miller)are members of the associa-
tion as well as City residents, devoted to the same ends. Plaintiffs

herein acted in the capacity of "private attorneys general"

in the classic sense, and clearly within the intended scope of

C.C.P. §1021.5. The subsequent election of Plaintiffs Anderson

and Miller to the Scotts Valley City Council in June, 1984, does

not alter their posture at the time litigation was initiated

(April, 1983) and the result sought achieved.

wanted apparently II

$41,858.25 IS A REASONABLE
AMOUNT FOR AN AWARD OF
ATTORNEY'S FEES HEREIN

In Press v. Lucky Stores, Inc. (1983) 34 Cal.3d 311, 317-322,

193 Cal.Rptr. 900, the California Supreme Court clarified the

procedure for determining the appropriateness and amount of an

award under C.C.P. §1021.5. The proper procedure is first to

determine the appropriateness of an award by application of the

statutory factors set forth in C.C.P. §1021.5, and, if an award

is found appropriate, then to calculate the amount of attorney's

fees to be awarded.

The amount of the award is to be determined by following

the guidelines set forth in Serrano v. Priest (1977) 20 Cal.3d 25,

48-49 (hereinafter Serrano III), namely, determination first of

a "touchstone" or "lodestar" figure based on a "careful compilation

of the time spent and reasonable hourly compensation for each

attorney . . . involved . . . ", which figure may then be augmented

or diminished by using a multiplier after the Court has considered

other factors concerning the case. The factors which a trial court

may consider in adjusting the lodestar figure are set forth in

Serrano III, supra, at 49. Trial court discretion in determining

the amount of an attorney's fee award, based on the above method,

sections.question: Specifically, you asked whether the activities of your organization require you to file a Statement of Organization and campaign disclosure reports pursuant to the Act. In your letter, you state that the purpose of your organization is to oppose a rezoning proposal for the Scotts Valley General Plan which is being considered by the city Council, and that there is no election in Scotts Valley during 1985, either to vote on the rezoning proposal or to elect members to the city council. You provided several pieces of literature which your organization has prepared and distributed to residents of Scotts Valley encouraging them to contact the city Council and attend Council meetings to voice opposition to the rezoning proposal. There is nothing in the literature which refers to or advocates the election or defeat of a candidate or measure. Finally, you asked whether you can include certain material in your pamphlets, such as copies of court documents and material out of the General Plan.
sections.conclusion: Because the materials you provided contain nothing that could be construed as expressly advocating the nomination, election or defeat of a candidate or measure, payments made by your organization are not 'expenditures.' Therefore, the payments your organization receives from its members are not 'contributions' and you have no obligation to file a statement of organization or campaign disclosure reports. The Commission has no authority to authorize or prohibit inclusion of such materials and I am not aware of any other law which would prohibit it. However, you may wish to contact your local district attorney for a definite answer.
sections.question_synthetic: Must an organization opposing a rezoning proposal file a Statement of Organization and campaign disclosure reports if they distribute materials encouraging residents to contact the city council, and can they include court documents and General Plan materials in their pamphlets?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The organization has no obligation to file a Statement of Organization because its activities do not constitute 'expenditures' under the Political Reform Act, as the materials do not expressly advocate for or against candidates or ballot measures. The FPPC cannot authorize or prohibit inclusion of court documents or General Plan materials.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains FPPC advice letter (A-85-214) dated October 28, 1985, followed by the incoming request from Warburton dated October 10, 1985, and supporting court documents. Extraction focuses on the FPPC response letter which contains clear Q&A sections. The incoming request and court documents are supplementary materials. High confidence in extraction due to clear structure and explicit conclusion language in the FPPC response.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Specifically, you asked whether the activities of your organization require you to file a Statement of Organization and campaign disclosure reports pursuant to the Act. In your letter, you state that the purpose of your organization is to oppose a rezoning proposal for the Scotts Valley General Plan which is being considered by the city Council, and that there is no election in Scotts Valley during 1985, either to vote on the rezoning proposal or to elect members to the city council. You provided several pieces of literature which your organization has prepared and distributed to residents of Scotts Valley encouraging them to contact the city Council and attend Council meetings to voice opposition to the rezoning proposal. There is nothing in the literature which refers to or advocates the election or defeat of a candidate or measure. Finally, you asked whether you can include certain material in your pamphlets, such as copies of court documents and material out of the General Plan.

CONCLUSION: Because the materials you provided contain nothing that could be construed as expressly advocating the nomination, election or defeat of a candidate or measure, payments made by your organization are not 'expenditures.' Therefore, the payments your organization receives from its members are not 'contributions' and you have no obligation to file a statement of organization or campaign disclosure reports. The Commission has no authority to authorize or prohibit inclusion of such materials and I am not aware of any other law which would prohibit it. However, you may wish to contact your local district attorney for a definite answer.

Section 18225 states that an "expenditure" is a payment made to or for the benefit of a candidate or committee, or a payment for communications which "expressly advocate the nomination, election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or candidates, or the qualification, passage or defeat of a clearly identified ballot measure." embedding.summary: The FPPC advises John David Warburton's organization opposing a Scotts Valley rezoning proposal that they are not required to file campaign disclosure reports because their materials and activities do not expressly advocate for or against any candidates or measures. The FPPC also notes it has no authority over the inclusion of court documents or General Plan materials in the organization's pamphlets.
